title: Build Grove MVP as a beads-backed Claude orchestration runtime
description:
Create the top-level umbrella epic for Grove as a beads-backed autonomous execution runtime that reuses `br` for task graph state, `bv` for graph analytics, and `claude` for session execution while keeping Grove-native ownership of runtime state, checkpoints, reservations, archive retrieval, and memory.

Why this matters:
- The repository already has a strong README and a much deeper PLAN.md; this epic gives future contributors a single source of intent inside the issue graph so the backlog itself explains the product boundary and avoids drift back toward a second task tracker or a wrapper around many unrelated CLIs.

## Success Criteria
See the dedicated acceptance criteria field.
design:
Planned scope:
    - Represent the whole product direction as a navigable epic that future work can hang from.
- Anchor the six implementation phases plus cross-cutting work under one visible backlog root.
- Make the backlog readable on its own so a future session can understand why Grove exists and what it must not become.
acceptance_criteria:
Done when:
- The backlog defines the product boundary and phase order using labels, comments, and explicit `blocks` dependencies rather than Beads parent links that distort readiness.
- Tracking epics are kept out of the executable ready lane while real implementation tasks remain dispatchable.
- A future contributor can inspect this issue and understand the over-arching product goal without reopening the design docs first.
notes:
Future-self context:
- This is a tracking epic and narrative anchor, not an autonomous dispatch candidate.
- After backlog review, hierarchy is represented by phase labels, stable IDs, comments, and explicit `blocks` edges because Beads parent-child semantics were found to block readiness.
- Keep the backlog executable, inspectable, and phase-gated; do not collapse this into vague umbrella work that future sessions must rediscover.

title: Establish architecture guardrails for ownership boundaries and strict phase sequencing
description:
Capture the non-negotiable design rules from PLAN.md and AGENTS.md directly in the backlog so implementation stays aligned with the confirmed direction.

Why this matters:
- The biggest risk at this stage is architectural drift: accidentally re-implementing task tracking, depending on external memory/orchestration tools, or breaking the deliberate phase order before the kernel is stable.
design:
Planned scope:
    - Record the ownership split: `br` authoritative for issues/dependencies/lifecycle, `bv` authoritative for analytics/triage, `claude` authoritative for execution backend.
- Record the complementary Grove-native responsibilities: runtime DB state, transcript archive, prompt materialization, reservations, playbook memory, crash recovery, and orchestration policy.
- Record the phase-order rule: kernel before session runtime, session runtime before parallel orchestration, orchestration before archive retrieval, archive before playbook, playbook before richer curation.
acceptance_criteria:
Done when:
    - The issue graph itself makes the ownership split explicit.
- Future work has a clear reminder of what must not be rebuilt or outsourced.
- The backlog preserves strict phase sequencing instead of allowing premature later-phase implementation.
notes:
Future-self context:
- This is a guardrail task, not a code feature. Its value is preventing wasted effort and making the issue graph safe to execute autonomously.
- This issue is intentionally shaped to preserve the architecture boundary from PLAN.md: `br` owns issue graph state, `bv` owns analytics, `claude` is the execution backend, and Grove owns orchestration/runtime state, memory, reservations, and recovery.
- Keep the backlog executable, inspectable, and phase-gated; do not collapse this into vague umbrella work that future sessions must rediscover.
close_reason: Guardrails encoded in revised backlog

title: Phase 6 - Rich curation
description:
Refine the playbook into a compact, self-correcting memory system with diaries, deduplication, conflict handling, anti-pattern inversion, and more nuanced selector behavior.

Why this matters:
- By this point Grove already works. This phase exists to keep the memory system healthy over time so long-lived use does not degrade prompt quality or encode harmful habits.

## Success Criteria
See the dedicated acceptance criteria field.
design:
Planned scope:
    - Add richer evidence signals from diaries and outcomes.
- Merge and deduplicate related rules while retaining useful support.
- Detect conflicts and invert harmful rules into explicit avoid-pattern guidance.
acceptance_criteria:
Done when:
    - The playbook remains compact instead of sprawling.
- Harmful or contradicted rules can be demoted or inverted into anti-pattern guidance.
- Selector behavior remains explainable and budget-aware over time.
notes:
Future-self context:
- This phase is about long-term memory quality, not first-use viability. Do it after the earlier promises are already true.
- This issue is intentionally shaped to preserve the architecture boundary from PLAN.md: `br` owns issue graph state, `bv` owns analytics, `claude` is the execution backend, and Grove owns orchestration/runtime state, memory, reservations, and recovery.
- Keep the backlog executable, inspectable, and phase-gated; do not collapse this into vague umbrella work that future sessions must rediscover.

title: Define release-readiness criteria for crash safety, observability, and operator trust
description:
Capture what 'safe enough to leave unattended' means for Grove so future work is judged against operator trust instead of just feature count.

Why this matters:

title: Implement dispatch eligibility policy and suppress non-executable ready items
description:
Define Grove's local dispatch-eligibility policy so the runtime can distinguish `ready in br` from `safe to dispatch in grove`, suppressing non-executable umbrella/tracking issues without inventing readiness of its own.

Why this matters:
- The plan says `br` is the sole authority for dependency satisfaction, but real backlogs can still contain epics or tracking issues that should never be sent to Claude. Grove needs an explicit suppress-only policy to keep the ready queue safe and understandable.

## Acceptance Criteria
See the dedicated acceptance criteria field.
design:
Planned scope:
- Define the local eligibility policy using Beads issue type and/or explicit labels such as `dispatch:no`.
- Distinguish `ready in br` from `dispatchable in grove` without ever manufacturing readiness locally.
- Surface structured suppression reasons that later status/inspect/scheduler code can reuse.
acceptance_criteria:
## Acceptance Criteria

Done when:
- Grove can represent `ready in br` and `dispatchable in grove` as separate concepts.
- Epics/tracking issues can safely appear in `br ready` without being dispatched.
- Suppression reasons are explicit, testable, and reusable by status/inspect and later scheduling code.
notes:
Future-self context:
- This issue exists because Beads hierarchy and real-world backlogs can contain non-executable items; Grove should suppress them explicitly instead of assuming every ready issue is a Claude task.
- The rule remains faithful to PLAN.md: Grove may only suppress readiness for local reasons; it must never create readiness independently of `br`.
- This policy is both a user-experience improvement and a safety rail for autonomous dispatch.
close_reason: Completed

comments.author: RedIsland
comments.text:

Why this issue exists in the backlog

comments.author: quangdang46
comments.text:
Implementation note from read-only prep on 2026-03-16:
- Actual `br` 0.1.12 JSON shapes in this repo differ from the wrapper schemas sketched in `PLAN.md`.
- `br ready --json` currently returns a bare JSON array of issue summaries (no outer `{ issues, count }` object in the live output I observed).
- `br list --status open --json` likewise returns a bare JSON array of summaries.
- `br show <id> --json` currently returns a one-element JSON array containing the full issue record, not a single JSON object.
- `br dep list <id> --json` currently returns an array of dependency rows with keys like `issue_id`, `depends_on_id`, `type`, `title`, `status`, and `priority`, not a `{ blocked_by, blocks }` object.
- `br ready --json` also writes informational logs to stderr in this environment, so Grove should parse stdout only and keep stderr for diagnostics.
- For `grove-1j9.5.5`, plan on a normalization layer around real CLI output rather than assuming the `PLAN.md` pseudotypes verbatim.

title: Implement graceful coordinator shutdown and visible stop-reason semantics
description:
Implement the coordinator shutdown sequence from the plan so Grove can stop safely, preserve runtime state, and leave behind clear operator-visible stop reasons instead of looking like a crash.

Why this matters:
- Users trust unattended systems more when they can interrupt them safely and understand what happened afterwards. The plan already defines a shutdown sequence; this task makes that sequence an explicit product deliverable.

## Acceptance Criteria
See the dedicated acceptance criteria field.
design:
Planned scope:
- Handle SIGINT/SIGTERM by setting a shutdown signal, halting new dispatch, and giving running sessions a grace period.
- Persist pending state, emit shutdown/interruption events, release the leader lease, and distinguish user stop from crash or generic failure.
- Make stop reasons queryable so status/inspect/log flows can explain what happened after shutdown or restart.
acceptance_criteria:
Done when:
- Grove can stop without dispatching new work and without silently losing pending state.
- Running sessions follow a graceful-stop path with a bounded grace period before forced termination.
- Operators can tell the difference between user-stopped, interrupted, crashed, and otherwise failed runs after the fact.
notes:
Future-self context:
- Shutdown is not the same thing as crash recovery; users need predictable termination semantics as well as restart reconciliation.
- This task should follow the explicit shutdown sequence in PLAN.md instead of improvising ad hoc signal handling.
- Making stop reasons durable improves both user trust and later retry/recovery behavior.
close_reason: Completed

comments.author: RedIsland
comments.text:
Background and intent
- The plan includes a concrete shutdown sequence: stop new dispatch, let sessions drain briefly, persist state, release lease, and exit cleanly.
- This task elevates that behavior into a first-class backlog item because graceful shutdown is a major part of operator trust.

Why this issue exists in the backlog
- It improves the user experience before a restart even happens.
- It keeps user-triggered interruption distinct from failure/crash semantics.
- It makes later status, inspect, and retry flows much easier to reason about.

acceptance_criteria:

title: Implement recovery capsules for failed, interrupted, and checkpointed runs
description:
Create compact, durable recovery artifacts that explain what happened on a failed or interrupted run and what the next attempt should do differently.

Why this matters:
- Recovery capsules are a high-leverage design element because they help both humans and later retries avoid rediscovering the same failure story from raw transcripts alone.

## Acceptance Criteria
See the dedicated acceptance criteria field.
design:
Planned scope:
    - Summarize the strongest evidence from a failed/interrupted run.
- Record likely root causes, risky paths, and what should not be repeated blindly.
- Make the capsules usable by retry flows and inspect surfaces.
- Persist a compact retry delta explanation when the next attempt changes framing, context selection, reservation scope, or verification order.
acceptance_criteria:
Done when:
    - Failed and interrupted work leaves behind a structured recovery summary.
- Retry logic and humans have a concise artifact to consult before reattempting.
- Retry or resume paths can explain how the next attempt differs from the previous one.
- Inspect/debug flows become faster and more reliable than raw log spelunking.
notes:
Future-self context:
- This task is about preserving hard-won debugging context in a durable, resumable form.
- This issue is intentionally shaped to preserve the architecture boundary from PLAN.md: `br` owns issue graph state, `bv` owns analytics, `claude` is the execution backend, and Grove owns orchestration/runtime state, memory, reservations, and recovery.
- Keep the backlog executable, inspectable, and phase-gated; do not collapse this into vague umbrella work that future sessions must rediscover.
close_reason: Enriched RecoveryCapsule root-cause fields and wired with_retry_context metadata preservation

title: Extend CLI with `grove run`, `grove log`, `grove retry`, and operator explainability surfaces
description:
Expose the orchestrator's real operator interface so users can launch autonomous work, follow live progress, inspect why Grove made certain decisions, and retry failed beads intentionally.

Why this matters:
- By Phase 3 the internals matter less than whether operators can actually drive and understand the runtime. This task makes the orchestrator legible from the CLI rather than forcing users to infer behavior from raw tables or transcripts.

## Acceptance Criteria
See the dedicated acceptance criteria field.
design:
Planned scope:
- Implement grove run, grove log, and grove retry.
- Enhance grove status and grove inspect to surface slot usage, dispatch reasons, lifecycle events, checkpoints, recovery state, stop reasons, and mirror state.
- Make grove log and grove inspect useful for event timelines, prompt/context provenance, retry-delta explanation, and machine-readable output contracts rather than only transcript dumping.
acceptance_criteria:
Done when:
- Operators can start, follow, inspect, and retry orchestrated work from the CLI.
- grove log can show transcript plus meaningful structured lifecycle events and retry/resume boundaries.
- grove status and grove inspect explain why work ran, paused, stopped, waited, or remained mirror-pending.
- The main operator surfaces have stable structured output shapes suitable for later JSON mode instead of ad-hoc terminal-only summaries.
notes:
Future-self context:
- Good operator ergonomics are part of runtime safety because invisible systems are harder to trust and recover.
- This task should make the orchestrator understandable without requiring DB spelunking.
- Think in terms of 'what would a user want to know next?' when shaping these surfaces.

title: Add operational schema addendum for coordinator lease, prompt provenance, dispatch decisions, config snapshots, and integrity checks
description:
Implement the highest-value operational tables from PLAN.md Section 21 and the explainability plumbing that makes prompt/config/dispatch provenance inspectable instead of mysterious.

Why this matters:
- The base schema is enough for a toy runtime, but the addendum tables are what make a production-oriented orchestrator inspectable after things go wrong. This issue deliberately elevates those tables into first-class backlog work because user trust depends on them being visible, not merely persisted.

## Acceptance Criteria
See the dedicated acceptance criteria field.
design:
Planned scope:
- Add coordinator lease state so leadership and takeover conditions are visible and durable.
- Add prompt materialization and dispatch-decision tables so the runtime can explain what it asked Claude, what context it injected, and why a bead was or was not dispatched.
- Add config snapshots and integrity-check records so historical behavior can be debugged against the actual runtime configuration and sanity checks in effect.
acceptance_criteria:
Done when:
- Leadership, prompt provenance, dispatch reasoning, config history, and integrity checks are durable and queryable.
- `grove inspect`/status flows have enough structured data to explain major autonomous decisions without raw-log archaeology.
- Grove's autonomy becomes inspectable rather than magical or opaque.
notes:
Future-self context:
- This task serves the long-term goal of operator trust.
- When autonomy surprises us, these tables are how we explain it.
- If provenance is captured but not surfaced, the user-value of this work is only half-realized.
close_reason: Added ops.rs schema for ops metadata. Wired into inspect models.

title: Phase 4 - Native archive and lexical retrieval

Why this matters:
- The plan is explicit that Grove must not depend on an external session-search CLI at runtime. This phase fulfills that promise and makes handoffs richer than one-hop parent summaries alone.

- Normalize session artifacts into searchable records.
- Use retrieved snippets to enrich later prompt assembly with bounded budgets.
acceptance_criteria:
Done when:
    - Completed task transcripts become searchable in Grove's own runtime state.
- Later tasks can retrieve relevant prior snippets natively.
- The architecture no longer relies on external search tooling for session reuse.

title: Add archive watermarks and idempotent resume semantics for incremental ingest
description:
Make transcript ingest restart-safe and idempotent by tracking what has already been processed and where ingestion should resume.

Why this matters:
- Operational completeness matters here: without watermarks, archive ingest after crashes or repeated runs risks duplication, omission, or ambiguous state.

## Acceptance Criteria
See the dedicated acceptance criteria field.
design:
Planned scope:
    - Track per-source ingest position or equivalent watermark state.
- Support safe resumption after interruption without duplicating archive records.
- Keep the ingest state transparent enough to debug when archive freshness is questioned.
acceptance_criteria:
Done when:
    - Archive ingest can resume safely after interruption.
- The runtime has a durable notion of what transcript data has been processed.
- Archive correctness does not depend on lucky one-shot execution.
notes:

- This issue is intentionally shaped to preserve the architecture boundary from PLAN.md: `br` owns issue graph state, `bv` owns analytics, `claude` is the execution backend, and Grove owns orchestration/runtime state, memory, reservations, and recovery.
- Keep the backlog executable, inspectable, and phase-gated; do not collapse this into vague umbrella work that future sessions must rediscover.

title: Implement observability enhancement for post-mortem analysis
description:
Enhance event logging with structured observability fields for post-run debugging and learning - NOT for real-time human intervention.

Why this matters:
- Grove runs unattended, so observability is for understanding what happened AFTER the fact, not for mid-run intervention. Structured logs enable better debugging and learning.

## Design
Planned scope:
- Enhance EventLogRecord with: correlation_id, operation, outcome (Success/Failure/Partial), duration_ms, error_class.
- Implement RunMetrics aggregation for 'grove inspect' command.
- Create RunReport struct with: total_duration, checkpoints_taken, retries_attempted, rescue_injections, termination_reason.
- Add ContextSnapshot field to capture state at event time for debugging.

## Acceptance Criteria
Done when:
- Events have structured outcome classification (not just 'error').
- Run reports summarize what happened without requiring raw log spelunking.
- Correlation IDs link related events across beads/runs.
- NO real-time alerting or human notification - observability is post-mortem only.

## Notes
Autonomous-first design:
- Observability is for debugging and learning, not monitoring dashboards.
- No alerts, no notifications, no real-time intervention hooks.
- Focus on structured data that helps future runs learn from past runs.

Future-self context:
- This is phase 3 observability that supports the autonomous recovery system.
- This issue is intentionally shaped to preserve the architecture boundary from PLAN.md.

title: Implement agent activity state detection and autonomous response mapping
description:
Detect what an agent is DOING (not just run status) and map each activity to an autonomous action without human intervention.

Why this matters:
- Grove's goal is unattended execution. Activity state lets us detect 'idle but not failed' and 'blocked but not exited' to trigger proactive autonomous rescue instead of waiting for timeout.

## Design
Planned scope:
- Define AgentActivity enum: Active, Ready, Idle, Blocked, Exited (NO WaitingInput - Grove design avoids this state).
- Implement activity detection heuristics from Claude output patterns, file access timestamps, and process state.
- Map each activity to AutonomousAction: Continue, CheckpointOrHandoff, InjectRescuePrompt, RetryWithMutation, RecoverOrFail.
- Integrate with existing CircuitBreaker to use activity state for early rejection decisions.

## Acceptance Criteria
Done when:
- The orchestrator can distinguish 'agent doing nothing' from 'agent actively working' from 'agent process died'.
- Each activity state has a clear autonomous response with NO human notification.
- Stuck agents are detected BEFORE timeout-based failure.
- Activity state is persisted in TaskRunRecord for post-mortem analysis.

## Notes
Autonomous-first design:
- NO WaitingInput state - Grove should avoid requiring user input mid-run.
- NO NotifyUser action - all actions are autonomous.
- Activity detection should work from observable signals (file timestamps, Claude output patterns, process state).

Future-self context:
- This is proactive stuck detection that complements the reactive CircuitBreaker.
- This issue is intentionally shaped to preserve the architecture boundary from PLAN.md.

comments.author: quangdang46

title: Implement event-driven reactions system for autonomous recovery
description:
Create a configurable, event-driven reaction system that automatically responds to triggers (circuit open, no progress, mirror failed) with autonomous actions - NO human notification.

Why this matters:
- Grove needs configurable automation for failure modes instead of hardcoded recovery logic. Reactions make recovery strategies declarative and inspectable.

## Design
Planned scope:
- Define ReactionTrigger enum: CircuitOpen, NoProgress, AgentIdle, ContextPressureHigh, MirrorFailed.
- Define ReactionAction enum: InjectRescue, RetryWithMutation, ForceCheckpoint, EnqueueMirrorRetry, ScheduleBackoff (NO NotifyUser).
- Implement ReactionRule struct with trigger, action, auto=true, max_attempts, escalate_to.
- Load reactions from grove.toml config with sensible defaults.
- Wire reactions into the orchestrator tick loop for automatic response.

## Acceptance Criteria
Done when:
- Common failure triggers automatically invoke configured reactions.
- Reactions are configurable via grove.toml without code changes.
- NO reaction ever notifies a human - all actions are autonomous.
- Reaction history is logged for post-mortem analysis.

## Notes
Autonomous-first design:
- Reactions replace hardcoded retry logic with declarative rules.
- Escalation means 'try different strategy' not 'notify human'.
- Default reactions should cover: circuit_open → inject_rescue, no_progress_3x → retry_with_mutation, mirror_failed → enqueue_retry.

Future-self context:
- This is the heart of Grove's autonomous recovery philosophy.
- This issue is intentionally shaped to preserve the architecture boundary from PLAN.md.

comments.author: quangdang46
comments.text: Strict verification result: this remains open. Reaction types and a kernel reaction evaluator exist, but key triggers are still stubbed, config loading is still defaults-only, and end-to-end dispatch integration for autonomous reactions is incomplete.

title: Implement escalation tiers for progressive autonomous strategy
description:
Create a tiered escalation system where each failure triggers a progressively different autonomous strategy - NOT escalation to human.

Why this matters:
- When autonomous recovery fails, Grove should try different strategies (rescue prompt → mutation → switch model) rather than repeating the same action or giving up immediately.

## Design
Planned scope:
- Define EscalationTier enum: FirstAttempt, SecondAttempt, ThirdAttempt, FinalAttempt, GiveUp.
- Define MutationStrategy enum: NarrowClaimedPaths, DifferentArchiveSnippet, AlternativeBeadContract, ReduceContextWindow, SwitchModel.
- Implement EscalationPolicy with max_tiers and tier_actions mapping.
- Track current tier per run in TaskRunRecord.
- On GiveUp tier, create RecoveryCapsule with full context.

## Acceptance Criteria
Done when:
- Each retry attempt uses a different strategy based on escalation tier.
- Tier progression is logged and inspectable.
- GiveUp tier creates a recovery capsule for future learning.
- NO tier ever escalates to human notification.

## Notes
Autonomous-first design:
- Escalation = 'try harder/smarter' not 'ask for help'.
- Default escalation ladder: 1st=continue, 2nd=inject_rescue, 3rd=narrow_paths, 4th=switch_model, give_up=capsule.
- Tier is reset on successful progress.

Future-self context:
- This builds on rescue cards (grove-1j9.6.7) and recovery capsules (grove-1j9.7.7).
- This issue is intentionally shaped to preserve the architecture boundary from PLAN.md.
